coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-driven surface morphology transformation towards superhydrophobic poly(lactic acid) film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yunfeng
Sun, Xin
Xue, Bai
Zhou, Ying
Xie, Lan
Zheng, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Focused on the design and implementation of superhydrophobic surface of poly(lactic acid) (PLA) film, the novel morphology regulating strategy (i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation) is reported to fabricate CQDs-grafted-poly(D-lactic acid)/poly(L-lactic acid) (CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA) films with tailorable hierarchical micro-nano structures. The hierarchical structures of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films evolve from laminar protrusions to microspheres to petal-like structures and further to micro-nano sphere structures, only by adjusting the CQDs-g-PDLA content. 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film with lotus leaf-like microstructures exhibits a record-high contact angle of 168° and a rather small sliding angle of 7.8°, indicating the pretty superhydrophobicity. Constructing controllable hierarchical structures is key factor to attain the superhydrophobicity, instead of commonly introducing suitable nanoparticles or hydrophobization agents. In combination with the high porosity, 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film illustrates a maximal oil-absorption up to 9.7 g/g. This strategy provides a novel insight into designing and constructing superhydrophobic PLA materials for the enormous application potential in surface self-cleaning and oil-water separation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic fabrication process of carbon quantum dots grafted with PDLA (CQDs-g-PDLA). A one-step method was used to react the carboxyl groups of D-lactic acid with CQDs during the in-situ polymerization process of D-lactide.

Structural characterization and luminescence mechanism of corn stalks-ground-derived CQDs. (a) AFM and (b) 3D height images of CQDs. (c) Height curve along the yellow line in a. (d) UV−vis absorption spectrum, and (e) FTIR spectrum of CQDs. (f) fluorescence spectrum of CQDs in 360 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm, and 408 nm. (g) and (h) The TEM observation of CQDs.

(a) FTIR spectra and (b) Raman spectroscopy of pure PDLA and PDLA-g-PDLA. (c) The partially enlarged view of the deviation after the grafting reaction of D-lactide, and (d) The Raman spectroscopy of pure CQDs. (e) and (f) The fitted C 1 s XPS spectra of CQDs and CQDs-g-PDLA, respectively.
SEM observations of PLA surfaces. (a1-a3) bulk PLLA, (b1-b3), (c1-c3), (d1-d3), (e1-e3) and (f1-f3) stereo-complexed PLA with the CQDs-g-PDLA concentration of 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 wt%, respectively.

The size distribution of PLA protrusions. (a) (b), (c), and (d) stereo-complexed PLA with the CQDs-g-PDLA concentration of 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt%, respectively. (e) and (f) micro-level and nano-level protrusions of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA, respectively.
The detailed mechanism for the growth of hierarchical structures during the CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation.

Melting behaviors of PLA composite films. A and B are stereo-complexed PDLA/PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composites, respectively. (a) is neat PLLA; (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) are stereo-complexed PLA with 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 wt％ PDLA or CQDs-g-PDLA, respectively.

(a) and (b) Contact angles of PDLA/PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films, respectively. (c) Comparison of contact angle for PLA-based films in this study and previous reports. (d) The sliding angle of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films.
Self-cleaning performance in air of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film.

Separation of oil existing on the water surface with 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film (a) pure water, (b) oil (red layer), (c) during, and (d) after the oil/water separation process. (e) the porosity and (f) oil-absorption of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films.
Cross-section SEM observations of PLA composites. (a1-a3), (b1-b3), (c1-c3), (d1-d3) and (e1-e3) stereo-complexed PLA with the CQDs-g-PDLA concentration of 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 wt%, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-driven surface morphology transformation towards superhydrophobic poly(lactic acid) film

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Focused on the design and implementation of superhydrophobic surface of poly(lactic acid) (PLA) film, the novel morphology regulating strategy (i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation) is reported to fabricate CQDs-grafted-poly(D-lactic acid)/poly(L-lactic acid) (CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA) films with tailorable hierarchical micro-nano structures. The hierarchical structures of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films evolve from laminar protrusions to microspheres to petal-like structures and further to micro-nano sphere structures, only by adjusting the CQDs-g-PDLA content. 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film with lotus leaf-like microstructures exhibits a record-high contact angle of 168° and a rather small sliding angle of 7.8°, indicating the pretty superhydrophobicity. Constructing controllable hierarchical structures is key factor to attain the superhydrophobicity, instead of commonly introducing suitable nanoparticles or hydrophobization agents. In combination with the high porosity, 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film illustrates a maximal oil-absorption up to 9.7 g/g. This strategy provides a novel insight into designing and constructing superhydrophobic PLA materials for the enormous application potential in surface self-cleaning and oil-water separation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As early as 1603, in the Song dynasty, an ancient Chinese poem describes the “lotus effect”: “how stainless it rises from its slimy bed!” [1]. This peculiar phenomenon that the lotus leaf surface is able to be thoroughly purified with moving water has invariably attracted numerous researchers. Barthlott and Neinuhuis reported the hydrophobicity was of relevance to the geomorphology of a plant leaf surface, which was dependent on high surface roughness stemming from the epicuticular wax and micro-papillae [2]. With further research progress, Jiang et al. first declared that the micro-nano architectures on the surface of lotus leaf, that is, the branched nanostructures at the top of the micropapillary, were the critical factor for the surface super-hydrophobic properties [3]. These micro/nano-structures are appropriately complied with the Cassie wetting model in theoretical frameworks of surface science. An air cushion formed by the entrapped air in micro/nano-structures results in the largely decreasing contact surface between water droplet and rough surface, accompanied by the spherical droplets appearing on the solid surface [4].

In recent years, hydrophobic poly (lactic acid) (PLA) materials have drawn abundant attention by reason of their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, environmental friendliness, and satisfactory mechanical properties [5–7]. Compared with hydrophobic petroleum-based materials, hydrophobic PLA can alleviate the secondary pollution to the environment, when it is used in the areas of oil-water separation, self-cleaning, and anti-icing [8–11]. Up to date, the methods for preparing hydrophobic PLA materials via building rough micro/nano-structures or reducing the surface energy are categorized into spray deposition, sol-gel, electrospinning, and template-assisted methods [12–15]. Regretfully, most the methods are expensive and/or detrimental to the ecosystem. For example, the sol-gel method generally needs low surface energy additives such as fluorosilicone to achieve the superhydrophobility, doing harm to the environment. The spray deposition method disperses functional coating into a great deal of organic solvent, which heavily pollutes the environment. The electrospinning and template-assisted methods are rather time- and energy-consuming processes. Moreover, PLA, one semi-crystalline polymer with a slow crystallization rate [16], is conducive to spherulite formation, restricting the construction of multi-scale structures. Therefore, a convenient and feasible method is urgently needed to improve the crystalline properties of PLA and construct surface micro/nano-structures. Fabricating micro-nano structures from stereo-complexed crystals (SCs) to obtain hydrophobic PLA materials timely appears in the shared vision, which is regarded as a promising technology for advanced materials.

Since it was reported in 1987 that SCs could be formed between PLLA and PDLA, this unique crystallization behavior has gradually attracted widespread concern [17,18]. Is it possible to control the crystallizing behaviors of PLA through PLLA/PDLA blends and to establish SCs protrusions for governing the hydrophobicity [19]? Nevertheless, it’s worth mentioning that the SCs will rapidly improve the homogeneous nucleation of adjacent PLA chains, which hinders and reduces the helical pairs stereo-complexed by PLLA and PDLA and finally decreases the crystallinity of SCs [20]. In other words, the nucleation efficiency of SCs is dominated by homogeneous nucleation rather than heterogeneous nucleation. How to further improve the nucleation efficiency of SCs is a great puzzle for material scholars. A tremendous amount of effort has been devoted to exploring the crystallization and hydrophobicity performance of PLLA/PDLA blend by introducing nanofillers such as silver nanoparticles [21], carbon nanotubes [22], graphene oxide [23], and TiO2 [24]. However, the intrinsic oleophobic and hydrophilic properties of nanofillers make them difficult to disperse into organic solvent. Therefore, improving the dispersibility of nanofillers in polymer solution is a crucial factor for enhancing the nucleation efficiency of SCs. Zhang et al. eliminated interface incompatibility between GO and PLLA by grating GO with PDLA to reduce the activation energy of crystallization, and ultimately obtained the high crystallinity of SCs [25]. Liu et al. synthesized MWCNTs-g-PDLA by in-situ polymerization, and then melting-mixed MWCNTs-g-PDLA with PLLA. The PLLA and MWCNTs-g-PDLA chains tended to line up side by side at their interface to construct the helical pairs, ultimately, co-crystallized into abundant SCs [26]. With these in mind, we considered whether carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could be grafted with PDLA (CQDs-g-PDLA) as an efficient SCs nucleating agent. Compared with other nanofillers, CQDs possess the remarkable advantages of ultra-low size, unique surface activity, and sufficient oxygen-containing functional groups, etc [27–30], thereby providing highly active functional groups for further modification of molecular structures. As expected, CQDs-g-PDLA has great potential in adjusting the SCs of PLA materials. Unfortunately, none of the stere-complexed crystalline CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA materials have been described in the previous literature.

In this study, biomass CQDs were fabricated from corn stalks using a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, and then successfully grafted with PDLA via a simple in-situ polymerization to prepared CQDs-g-PDLA. Finally, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films were fabricated by using a novel CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation approach. CQDs-g-PDLA can not only improve heterogeneous nucleation but also enhance SC nucleation efficiency, so that it can effectively control the phase separation process of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA solution. Hence, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films with tunable hierarchical micro-nano structures (laminar protrusions, microspheres, petal-like structures, and micro-nano sphere structures) were conveniently constructed by simply regulating the CQDs-g-PDLA content. The hierarchical micro-nano structures are key factors to enable the superhydrophobicity of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films. It is worth noting that the contact angles are sharply increased from 126° for bulk PLLA film to the record-high 168° for 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film that has extremely lotus leaf-like micro-nano sphere protrusions. The CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation approach has made a huge progress towards green superhydrophobic PLA films, which has enormous implication for self-cleaning and oil-water separation materials.

The corn stalks obtained from Lianfeng Co. Ltd., China, were used as the carbon source. D-lactide (C6H8O4) with a molecular weight of 144.13 and a purity of greater than 90% was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). PLLA of trademark 4032D with an
M w
̅
of 2.7 × 105 and a density of 1.27 × 103 kg/m3 was procured from Nature Works (America). All reagents of analytical grade were utilized as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot solution
ce:section.ce:para: The corn stalks were disintegrated and carbonized using microwave process (700 W), secondarily went through the strong acid hydrothermal reaction for being oxidized and stripped into CQDs. The detailed operation procedure is as follows: Firstly, the corn stalks (3 g) were immersed in 80 ml dilute H2SO4 solution (1 wt%) under ultrasonic dispersion for 25 min. Afterward, the dispersion was put in the microwave for 5 min, and then poured into the hydrothermal reactor to react at 185 °C for 5 h. Afterwards, the mixing dispersion was filtered to obtain CQDs solution. This time-saving, energy-saving, and environmentally friendly method has great potential in producing biomass CQDs. The CQDs have a variety of surface functional groups and excellent water solubility, which is convenient for subsequent functional modification and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots grafted with poly (D-lactic acid)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs possess many oxygen-containing groups, while the synthesis of PDLA is mainly the condensation reaction of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in D-lactic acid. Hence, synthesizing CQDs-g-PDLA by the one-step method is feasible ( Fig. 1). Firstly, 20 g of D-lactide was placed into a three-necked round-bottom flask and subjected to vacuum distillation at 60 °C for 5 h to eliminate the moisture. After water elimination, the solution was transferred into a reaction bottle. Meanwhile, 10 ml of diluted CQDs solution by ethanol was added into it. And vacuum distillation was performed for another 4 h under the same conditions to remove the residual moisture. As the temperature was increased to 130 °C, stannous octoate was gradually dropped as catalyst and the mixture was reacted for 4 h. Then, the mixture was successively reacted at 150 °C for 10 h and at 165 °C for 24 h. The reactions at 130 °C and 150 °C were mainly the prepolymerization stages. Then, the products were dissolved in n-methyl-2-pyrrolidone and precipitated in ethanol. Finally, the products were centrifuged and dried in a vacuum oven to acquire CQDs-g-PDLA samples.

We applied the CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation method. The specific operation is as follows: Firstly, PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA commixture was dissolved in chloroform at 30 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA dispersion was stirred vigorously for half an hour to ensure the adequate contact between PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA. Secondly, 5 ml the mixing dispersion was poured into a glass petri dish. Then, ethanol (3 ml) was slowly dropped into the solution as a non-solvent. The dispersion was immediately frozen at − 15 °C for 18 h, decreasing the solvent vaporization rate and facilitating crystal nucleation. Ultimately, the stereo-complexed PLA film was dried under vacuum overnight to eliminate remanent solvent.

An SEM (Nova Nano450) with operation accelerating voltage of 5 kV was applied to observe the surface morphology of porous CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA materials. All the samples were cryo-fractured and spray-coated with gold to elevate the resolution before observation.

The CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA porous film was monitored by a DSC Q2000 (TA Instruments, USA). The DSC heating traces were gathered by heating the films from 25° to 250 °C (5 °C/min) under the nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, the melt crystallization dynamics were measured in the cooling process (5 °C/min). Finally, the film was heated again under the same conditions. In addition, the crystallinity degree (HCs and SCs) is obtained from the Eq. (1).

An OCA20 machine (Data-Physics, Germany) was utilized to determine the sliding and contact angles on the surface of samples at ambient temperature. Additionally, each sample was explored three times, and the mean value was taken as the result.
The FTIR spectra of CQDs-g-PDLA and pure PDLA materials prepared under the same conditions were recorded on an FTIR IS50 (Nicolet, USA) with 16 scans.

XPS spectra were acquired on a Thermo Scientific spectrometer (K-alpha, ThermoFisher) with a monochromated Al Kα source. The pass energy of the analyzer used to acquire the broad spectrum and individual photoelectron line was 100 and 50 eV, respectively.
The Raman spectra of CQDs, pure PDLA, and CQDs-g-PDLA were acquired on a Raman spectroscope (Renishaw inVia) with a 514 nm laser wavelength.
The UV–vis spectroscopy characterization was carried out with a Hitachi UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Japan).
A Horiba Fluoromax 4 spectrophotometer was employed to obtain fluorescence spectrum under different excitation wavelengths.
A Bruker Dimension ICON was used to image the CQDs dispersed on the mica substrate. The image was acquired in tapping mode.
The morphology of CQDs was also observed by using TEM (ZEISS Gemini 300, 80 kV).

Fig. 2 provides insights into the microstructures, chemical composition, and fluorescence properties of the bio-based CQDs originated from corn stalks. According to the AFM images (Fig. 2a-c), the CQDs are well distributed with an average size around 16 nm, in good agreement with the TEM observations (Fig. 2g and h). Particularly, the thickness of CQDs is within a narrow region of 1–2 nm, which is most likely to reveal the slight assemblies of multiple tiny nanosheets [31]. It is notable that the diameter of the CQDs is a lot larger than the height. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 2d), a sharp band observed at 291 nm is ascribed to n-π * transition of CO bonds [32]. Conspicuously, the electronic structures of CQDs are greatly complied with the molecular orbital model [33–35]. The π-state atoms in CQDs may be stemmed from the sp2-hybridized aromatic-C in their core [36]. The carbonyl functional groups can build a bond with the sp2-hybridized aromatic-C through the lone electron pair. The electron can be transferred from the carbonyl group state n to the aromatic ring state π * (n→π * ) [28,37]. Meanwhile, this result can be further confirmed from the strong characteristic CO FTIR absorption peak at 1722 cm−1 (Fig. 2e) and the optimal excitation wavelength at 408 nm (Fig. 2f). In addition, the defects on CQDs surface and carbonyl groups bonded on aromatic rings are able to be co-hybridized to form the defective surface state. The photoluminescence of CQDs results from the radiation transitions on the defective surface. Similar to other reported CQDs, these CQDs also display the emission largely influenced by excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength is elevated from 360 to 408 nm, the wavelength of maximum fluorescence intensity shifts towards a larger value, owing to the different sizes and multi-surface emission sites of CQDs. The prepared CQDs are a well-dispersed yellow solution under natural light, while exhibiting green fluorescence under ultraviolet light of 365 nm (Fig. S1).

The FTIR spectra of CQDs-g-PDLA can reveal the interaction between PDLA and CQDs, helping distinguish the segment mobility and polar interactions. In the methyl stretch region of the FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 3a), the absorption peaks of asymmetric (2995 cm−1) and symmetric (2947 cm−1) CH3 stretching vibration in pure PDLA are slightly increased to 2999 and 2948 cm−1 for CQDs-g-PDLA, respectively [38]. Meanwhile, the absorption band at 1455 cm−1 due to CH3 deformation vibration is blue-shifted to 1457 cm−1, which means that the conformational ordering degree has increased [39]. This blue shift is on account of the intense interactions between PDLA and CQDs. Notably, the CO stretching band is observed at 1747 cm−1 in pure PDLA, while the peak is increased to 1758 cm−1 with the graft of CQDs [25]. The most probable reason for this blue shift is that the carbonyl or hydroxyl groups in PDLA and the oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs form strong hydrogen bonds. Additionally, new bands appearing at 2851 and 1282 cm−1 for CQDs-g-PDLA are ascribed to the introduction of CQDs [27]. The band 2851 cm−1 is presumably due to the CQDs-assisted formation of perfectly flat crystals, and the band 1282 cm−1 is assigned to CQDs-induced conformational ordering in the PLA chain backbone on expense of random coils [27].

To further analyze chemical structures of CQDs-g-PDLA and verify the result of FTIR, both XPS and Raman were applied to detailedly evaluate the samples. By comparing the Raman spectrum of CQDs-g-PDLA with those of neat PDLA and CQDs, the successful graft of PDLA on CQDs is proved (Fig. 3b-d) [40]. The absorption bands at 711, 760, and 1733 cm−1 are indicative of CO bonds in pure PDLA (Fig. 3b). Moreover, the peak at 1179 cm−1 corresponds to C-O asymmetric stretching, whereas the absorption peak centered at 1092 cm−1 is ascribed to C-O-C symmetric stretching. Furthermore, the CH3 asymmetric stretching peak is viewed at 1450 cm−1, and the peaks indexed to CH3 symmetric stretching are located at 1128 and 1386 cm−1. According to the Raman spectrum of CQDs-g-PDLA (Fig. 3c), we observe new absorption bands at 1541 and 1660 cm−1, which are the characterized absorption peaks of CQDs. Compared with the Raman spectra of the prepared CQDs (Fig. 3d), it is obviously found that D band and G band are largely shifted to higher wavenumber after grafting, suggesting the strong interaction between PDLA chains and CQDs [27]. As shown in Fig. 3e, three peaks can be observed at 285.0, 286.3, and 288.1 eV in the CQDs XPS spectrum. Particularly, the band at 285.0 eV binding energy is identified as C-C/C-H of CQDs; the key peak at 286.3 eV represents C-OH, which can take part in the ring-opening polymerization of D-lactide [41]; the peak existing at 288.1 eV indicates carbonyl groups (CO). The three peaks attributed to CO (287.1 eV), C-OH (286.6 eV), and C-C/C-H (285.0 eV) are also found in the fitted C 1 s spectrum of CQDs-g-PDLA (Fig. 3f). Note that a new strong band centered at 289.3 eV is indexed to O-CO, with the grafting PDLA. What’s more, the intensity of C-OH peak is significantly reduced after the grafting, which is due to the fact that C-OH on CQDs is consumed as a reactant for the ring-opening polymerization of D-lactide during the grafting process. Thereby, one can make a conclusion that PDLA is successfully grafted on CQDs, in combination with the analyses of FTIR and Raman.

Surface morphology is a critical factor for super-hydrophobicity of materials [42]. It is significant to adjust the processing parameters for tailoring surface structures of PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA blends. As depicted in Fig. 4, SEM images illustrate the surface topography characteristic of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composites. The surface of pure PLLA film exhibits a few laminar protrusions in micron level (Fig. 4a), which is in sharp contrast with the stereo-complex PLA at different contents of CQDs-g-PDLA. Fig. 4a3 indicates that fish-scale nano-networks spread all over the PLLA surface, in accordance with the previous report [43]. This phenomenon is attributed to liquid-liquid phase separation that contributes to the interconnected networks between polymer-lean and polymer-rich phases. Evolution in protrusion structures from lamella to microsphere is observed for 5 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite film (Fig. 4b). Essentially, the robust hydrogen interaction between CQDs-g-PDLA and PLLA results in the restrained time and mobility to construct large-sized and ordered lamella at a low temperature. These microspheres indicate about 1.2 µm in diameter ( Fig. 5a), and many of them agglomerate together to form larger convex bulk.

When the CQDs-g-PDLA content is increased to 10 wt%, intriguing petal-like structures accompanied by multi-layered nano-folds are demonstrated on the surface (Fig. 4c). The formation of these petal-like assemblies is of relevance to the occurrence of two distinct phase separation process, that is, fast liquid-solid phase separation triggered by rapid stereo-complexed crystallization of CQDs-g-PDLA and PLLA that generates plenty of micro-domains, followed by slow liquid-liquid phase separation around these micro-domains induced by the slower homogeneous crystallization of PLLA. It is considered that the prerequisites for these petal-like assemblies are faster stereo-complexed crystallization, slower homogeneous crystallization, and strong intermolecular interaction [44,45]. Afterwards, the size of petal-like crystal assemblies is reduced from 12.1 µm for 10 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA (Figs. 5b) to 7.4 µm for 15 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA (Figs. 4d and 5c) and further to 4.4 µm for 20 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA (Figs. 4e and 5d). The enhanced heterogeneous nucleation possibility of SCs in CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA should be responsible for rapidly fabricating more numbers of smaller assemblies in the confined space. Generally speaking, crystal size and phase separation rate exhibit an opposite relation, in consistence with the report of Sun et al. [19]. Moreover, both the size and number of petal nanosheets are largely reduced with the continuous increase of CQDs-g-PDLA loading, which can be explained by the fact that the greatly increased SCs consume more PLLA chains around the micro-domains, attenuating liquid-liquid phase separation that is caused by homogeneous crystallization of PLLA.

At the highest content of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA (Fig. 4f), the multiple structures consisting of micro-level subround protrusion of about 6.1 µm and nano-level round protrusion of about 800 nm are formed (Fig. 5e and f), extremely approximating to the micro-nano structures of lotus leaf (Fig. S2). Numerous SC nucleuses generated at the high CQDs-g-PDLA content of 30 wt% would consume a large number of PLLA chains, which leads to the too low PLLA concentration to cause liquid-liquid phase separation around the micro-domains. Thus, the SCs grow in width and height during the liquid-solid phase separation, ultimately forming smooth spherical protrusions without any nanopetals. In addition, adjacent nucleuses growing independently might merge together into a large subround assembly at micro-level; other isolated nucleuses grow into a round nano-assembly around the micro-level protrusions.

The detailed mechanism for the growth of hierarchical structures during the CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation is schematically presented in Fig. 6. The hierarchical CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films are produced by phase separation that takes place while the free energy of single-phase system is lower than that of two-phase system at the identical conditions [46]. As the chloroform (solvent) begins to contact with ethanol (nonsolvent), concentration fluctuation occurs at the nonsolvent/solvent interface, owing to the polymer chain aggregation driven by the decrease of system free energy level, eliciting the diffusion-induced separation into polymer-lean and polymer-rich phase (liquid-liquid phase separation). Therefore, neat PLLA mixture solution with high free energy is facile to form polymer-rich phase and polymer-lean phase in the ternary PLLA-chloroform-ethanol system (Fig. 6a). Since chloroform far faster evaporates than ethanol, phase inversion (i.e., the isolated polymer-lean phase turns into continuous phase) takes place with the further volatilization of chloroform and diffusion of ethanol. The incremental viscosity and PLLA concentration in polymer-rich phase with the phase gathering during this process trigger liquid-liquid phase separation to build the fish-scale skeletons of porous materials. The trail of polymer-lean phase is kept in the 3D network structures, fabricating the nanopores through the fish-scale networks. Furthermore, a small quantity of HCs in the polymer-rich phase would grow into large-size lamellae on the PLLA surface via liquid-solid phase separation.

As depicted in Fig. 6b, helical pairs (nucleus precursors) are complexed by PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA through the powerful intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the introduction of 5 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA, which immensely confines the mobility of PLA chains. SC nucleuses are already constructed by the compactly neighboring pre-aligned PLA chains with the assistance of the complexation of PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA. Besides, the grafted CQDs as active foreign heterogeneities can enhance the heterogeneous nucleation and facilitate the formation of SCs. Some HC nucleuses close to SC domains are built from the chain entanglements by reason of the strong hydrogen bonds between PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA. The neighboring SC and HC nucleuses may be incorporated with each other and grow into the large-size convex. Natheless, the independent HC nucleuses far away from the SC nucleuses will grow by the folding of PLLA chains at the phase interface into smaller spheres.

With the increasing loading of CQDs-g-PDLA up to 10–20 wt% (Fig. 6c), much more SC nucleuses formed by pairs of CQDs-g-PDLA and PLLA consume enough PLLA chains to induce the liquid-liquid phase separation around the SC domains. Thus, SC-induced liquid-solid phase separation generates the core of petal-like crystals, and spinodal liquid-liquid phase separation contributes to the lamellae petals. When the CQDs-g-PDLA content increases to the highest 30 wt% (Fig. 6d), plenty of SC nucleuses consume so many PLLA chains that the PLLA concentration around the SC domains is too low to initiate the liquid-liquid phase separation. As a result, SC nucleuses grow into quite smooth spherical protrusions with no lamellae petals. Moreover, a lot of adjacent SC nucleuses growing independently might merge together into large-size subround protrusions; the others isolated from each other grow in width and height into round nanospheres around the large-size protrusions. Together, a series of hierarchical CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films were successfully fabricated by the CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation approach.

The microstructures of porous PLA materials are proved to be closely correlated to the crystalline behavior in recent discovery [47]. The multi-scale structures composed of micro-nano protrusions and pores on the surface of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films are mainly dependent on the crystallization behavior of PLA solution mixture. Compared with PDLA/PLLA nanocomposites, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA nanocomposites exhibit the significantly enhanced crystallization, because CQDs-g-PDLA as highly active nucleating agent triggers much more local SC nucleuses at the interface which are expected to largely accelerate the crystallization. So as to certify the high nucleating effectiveness of CQDs-g-PDLA, DSC was used to provide insights into the crystallization behaviors of PDLA/PLLA and CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films ( Fig. 7). Pure PLLA shows a single sharp endothermic peak at 168.5 °C with the crystallinity of merely 31.8%, corresponding to the fusion of HCs. After introducing 5 wt% PDLA (Fig. 7A), another weak melting peak observed at 212.9 °C is attributed to SCs, and the SC crystallinity is as low as 3.9% due to the small PDLA loading fraction. As expected, the SC crystallinity is continuously augmented with the incremental PDLA content. Detailedly, the SC crystallinity degree is enhanced from 11.9% for 10 wt% PDLA/PLLA to 35.6% for 30 wt% PDLA/PLLA. However, the HC crystallinity shows a reverse tendency to the evolution of SC crystallinity. The HC crystallinity of 30 wt% PDLA/PLLA is greatly reduced to only 6.1%, compared with that of 5 wt% PDLA/PLLA (20.7%). This stems from the competition between SCs and HCs, together with the SCs formation prioritizing HCs [48].

It can be easily found that CQDs-g-PDLA plays a similar role to pure PDLA in the crystallization of PLA matrix. The SC crystallinity is gradually elevated from 4.8% to 15.9%, 18.7%, 24.6%, and 41.4%, respectively (Fig. 7B), as the CQDs-g-PDLA loading fraction is increased from 5 wt% to 10, 15, 20, and 30 wt%. Furthermore, the SC crystallinity of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composites is much larger than that of PDLA/PLLA composites at the same loading fraction, which demonstrates that the stereo-complex nucleation efficiency of CQDs-g-PDLA is superior to that of PDLA. For instance, the SC crystallinity of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite (41.4%) is relatively larger than that of 30 wt% PDLA/PLLA composite (35.6%). It is worth noticing that the HC crystallinity of 30 wt% PDLA/PLLA composite is approaching to 0, signifying no more HC formation, which is associated with the results of Fig. 5f. Moreover, the melting point of SCs slightly rises with the increase of CQDs-g-PDLA content. Particularly, a unique shoulder SC melting peak is observed at 206.5 °C for 30% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite. This shoulder peak appearance could be assigned to the deficient SCs composed of thinner crystals at the interface. The numerous SC nucleuses lead to the limited time and mobility for the not-fully grown SCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface superwetting property and self-cleaning performance
ce:section.ce:para: The surface wettability (contact angle and sliding angle) is critical for self-cleaning applications of PLA materials [49]. It is believed that surface wettability is dominated by morphologies and surface energy [42]. However, PLA is a hydrophilic polymer that contains many polar groups (-COO-). Thus, the construction of surface micro-nano structures is a vital way to achieving superhydrophobic effect, without changing the chemical structures of PLA. In this work, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films with hierarchical structures comprised of micro- and nano-protrusions, quite similar to those of lotus leaf, are successfully fabricated by a straightforward CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation method, presenting surface super-hydrophobicity.

Fig. 8a illustrates the surface contact angle of PDLA/PLLA blend films with different PDLA content. As the PDLA content increases, the surface contact angle is largely improved from 126° to 152°. However, on the same content conditions, the contact angle of the CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite film is increased more significantly (Fig. 8b). Taking 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite as an example, the contact angle rises to a maximum value of 168°, which far surpasses that of 30 wt% PDLA/PLLA composite (152°). The multi-level micro-nano structures could help improving the surface hydrophobicity, according to the Cassie-Baxter theory [50]. The principle is that a great deal of air is trapped in surface pores of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite and the air cushion between PLA surface and water droplet is created, resulting in the largely decreasing contact area between water droplet and PLA surface [24]. Therefore, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite exhibits the significantly improved contact angle, and the record-high super-hydrophobic effect is achieved for PLA-based materials.

In addition, the comparison of contact angle for the prepared 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film with that of some other reported PLA-based films is enumerated in Fig. 8c. It is quite difficult to achieve an ultrahigh contact angle (>160°) for PLA films, due to plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups (OC-O) existing in PLA chains. In this investigation, it’s clearly seen that 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film exhibits the record-high contact angle of 168°, which prevails over all the reported hydrophobic PLA-based films [7,10,16,24,50,51]. The comparison results elucidate that this creative strategy is competitive and significant to fabricate superb superhydrophobic PLA-based films with the ultrahigh contact angle > 160°.

Sliding angle is another key parameter for hydrophobic materials. Water droplets on the PLA film surface have obviously demonstrated sliding phenomenon (Fig. 8d). The sliding angle of bulk PLLA film is up to 30°, whereas the sliding angle is sharply decreased to 14.8° with the introduction of 5 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA. Besides, the sliding angle is continuously reduced with the incremental CQDs-g-PDLA content, and the minimal value of 7.8° is achieved for 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film. In combination with the contact angle of 168°, 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film displays the record-high superhydrophobicity, which corresponds to the Cassie-Baxter model. The micro-level and nano-level protrusions of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA can greatly enhance the surface roughness. The water droplet cannot penetrate the inter of the gully, when it contacts the multi-level rough surface. An air cushion is simply created beneath the water droplet assisted by the entrapped air in micro-nanostructures, which contributes to the immensely decreased contact area of PLA surface with the water droplet. Predictably, the water droplet only has a point contact with the PLA surface and easily slides on the surface to reach super-hydrophobic effect.

Given the excellent water antiwettability on CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films, it is reasonable to expect that these films can be potentially used in the field of self-cleaning, especially repelling dust in air [52,53]. Next, we take tannic acid, Fe3O4, and SiO2 nanoparticles as dust to carry out the self-cleaning measurements for 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film ( Fig. 9). For clarity, the flushing water is dyed by Sunset Yellow. The simulative dust can be simply washed out by the moving water droplets and the film exhibits excellent water repellence, indicating the pretty self-cleaning performance in air of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film [54,55]. As a result, the obtained superhydrophobic CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films are promising substitutes for advanced engineering materials with great self-cleaning ability.

Simultaneously, hydrophobicity, oleophilicity, and high porosity are critical factors for porous oil-water separation material [56–58]. The CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films exhibit fantastic water antiwettability and high porosity stemming from the hierarchical microstructures, which is the prerequisite imposed by the oil-absorption [59,60]. As shown in Fig. 10e, the porosity of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films is slightly augmented from 85.4% to 89.5% with the incremental CQDs-g-PDLA content. In the phase separation procedure, the increasing CQDs-g-PDLA consume a mass of PLLA chains to form SC, contributing to the enhancing polymer-lean phase that evolves into pores. In virtue of high porosity (89.5%) and low density (0.130 g/cm3), 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film exhibit outstanding oil-water separation performance. As the 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film is encountered with red oil droplets dyed by oil-red on the water, the oil droplets can be absorbed completely into CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA porous films in thirty seconds with repelling water, indicating that the CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films are effective at oil-water separation (Fig. 10a-d). The oil-absorption has a generally consistent variation trend with the porosity (Fig. 10f). Notably, 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film illustrates a maximal oil-absorption up to 9.7 g/g. Simultaneously, to more directly characterize the internal pore state and changes, the cross-section SEM observations of PLA composites were performed ( Fig. 11). As the CQDs-g-PDLA content increases, the improvement of nucleation ability promotes more stable network structure generated by polymer-rich phase in solution. Thus, the volume constructed by polymer-lean phase can be reserve well during the phase separation, leading to an increase of porosity [11,61]. The infiltrated oil is primely physically stored in pores, originated from capillary effect. Together, the fabricated superhydrophobic CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films has great potential as promising oil-water separation materials.

In summary, high-value biomass CQDs were successfully derived from agricultural waste (corn stalks) by a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, and then CQDs-g-PDLA was correspondingly synthesized using a simple in-situ polymerization of D-lactide. Ultimately, CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA composite films were creatively fabricated via a novel CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation approach. CQDs-g-PDLA can serve as a heterogeneous nucleating agent to increase crystal nuclei as well as enhance the SC nucleation efficiency. CQDs-g-PDLA exhibits the advantages of nontoxicity, nucleation efficiency, dispersibility, compatibility, and bioresource, in comparison with the traditional nucleating agents. Hence, the SC crystallinity of 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA is gradually increased to 41.4% with the incremental CQDs-g-PDLA content, while the HC crystallinity surprisingly approaches to zero. Accordingly, the surface morphology of CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA films can be tailored from laminar protrusions to microspheres to petal-like structures and further to micro-nano sphere structures by simply changing the CQDs-g-PDLA content. It is notable that 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA film with extremely lotus leaf-like micro-nano sphere protrusions exhibits the record-high contact angle of 168° and quite small sliding angle of 7.8°, on account of Cassie wetting theory. An air cushion formed by the entrapped air in the micro/nano-structures results in the decreasing contact surface between the water droplet and rough surface. Constructing controllable hierarchical structures is key factor to attain the superhydrophobicity, instead of commonly introducing suitable nanoparticles or hydrophobization agents, which possesses the superiority such as low cost, easy operation, adjustability, and versatility. Additionally, 30 wt% CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA possesses excellent self-cleaning ability and a maximal oil-absorption up to 9.7 g/g. The CQDs-nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation method sheds new light on fabricating and controlling superhydrophobic PLA materials for self-cleaning and oil-water separation in advanced smart materials.
